10-robotics/algorithms/Spatial-Algebra-a.title: Spatial algebra and the Articulated-Body Algorithm
10-robotics/algorithms/Spatial-Algebra-a.content:
Summary
Forward dynamics on a kinematic tree — given (q, \dot q, \tau, f^{ext}), solve for \ddot q — can be done in O(n) without ever forming the joint-space inertia M(q). The Articulated-Body Algorithm (ABA) propagates a 6×6 articulated inertia I^A_i and a 6-vector bias p^A_i through the tree such that the inboard force satisfies f_i = I^A_i a_i + p^A_i, then unfolds joint accelerations in a third sweep. The construction rests on (i) Plücker spatial algebra (c.f. Pluecker-coordinates) with its motion/force duality, (ii) the body-frame EOM I\dot\nu + \nu \times^* I\nu = \phi^{ext}, and (iii) an induction over the tree that eliminates each \ddot q_c via the joint constraint \tau_c = S_c^\top f_c at the joint frame.
Notation and Conventions
Reference textbook: Featherstone, Rigid Body Dynamics Algorithms, Springer 2008

Body indices i = 1,\dots, N; \lambda (i) is the parent of body i; body 0 is the root/world.
\nu_i, a_i are the spatial velocity and accelerations in body i‘s frame (c.f. spatial-coordinates).
f_i is the spatial force on body i from \lambda (i), transmitted via the joint i (body-i frame)
S_i \in \mathbb R^{6\times d_i} is the motion subspace of joint i (e.g. (0,0,1,0,0,0)^\top for a z-axis revolute joint).
\tau_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_i} is the active joint force (actuator torque).
{}^i X_j is the spatial motion transform from j to i.
{}^i X^*_j = ({}^j X_i)^\top is the dual force-vector transform.
I_i is the rigid-body spatial inertia of body i in its own frame.
\boldsymbol{c}_i = \nu_i \times S_i\,\dot q_i is the velocity-product bias acceleration. It appears because differentiating \nu_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\nu_{\lambda(i)} +    S_i\dot q_i in body coordinates produces this Coriolis-like term even when \ddot q_i = 0 and a_{\lambda(i)} = 0.
p_i = \nu_i \times^* I_i\,\nu_i - f^\text{ext}_i is the per-body bias force: the part of body i‘s Newton-Euler equation that doesn’t multiply a_i. It collects the gyroscopic/Coriolis wrench \nu \times^* I\nu and any external wrench applied to the body.

Core Mechanism
Spatial algebra primitives. A twist \nu = (\omega, v_O) \in \mathbb{R}^6 lives in motion space M^6; a wrench \phi = (n_O, f) \in \mathbb{R}^6 lives in dual force space F^6. Pairing \langle \phi, \nu \rangle = n_O^\top \omega + f^\top v_O is frame-invariant. Cross operators:
\nu\times = \begin{bmatrix} \hat\omega & 0 \\ \hat v_O & \hat\omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nu\times^* = \begin{bmatrix} \hat\omega & \hat v_O \\ 0 & \hat\omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad (\nu\times)^\top = -\nu\times^*.
Motion transforms {}^B X_A act on twists; force vectors use the dual {}^B X^*_A = ({}^A X_B)^\top.
Body-frame EOM. Newton’s law \dot h^0 = \phi^{ext,0} in the inertial frame pulls back via the structural identity
{}^0\dot X_B = {}^0 X_B\,(\nu^B \times),
which is the 6×6 adjoint analogue of R^\top \dot R = \hat\omega on SO(3). Transposing and dualizing yields {}^0\dot X^*_B = {}^0 X^*_B\,(\nu^B \times^*), hence the transport theorem for co-vectors:
\frac{d^0 h^0}{dt} = {}^0 X^*_B\Big( \frac{d^B h^B}{dt} + \nu^B \times^* h^B \Big).
Substituting h = I\nu with I constant in body coords gives
I\dot\nu + \nu \times^* I\nu = \phi^{ext}. \tag{NE}
The \nu\times^*I\nu term is not part of momentum — it is the correction for differentiating a co-vector in a frame that moves with the body. Restricting to pure rotation about the CoM (\nu = (\omega,0), I = \text{diag}(I_c, mI_3)) recovers I_c\dot\omega + \omega \times I_c \omega = \tau, the classical Euler equation.
Kinematic recurrence (Pass 1). For body i with parent \lambda(i):
\nu_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\,\nu_{\lambda(i)} + S_i \dot q_i, \quad a_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\, a_{\lambda(i)} + S_i \ddot q_i + \underbrace{\nu_i \times S_i \dot q_i}_{=: c_i}, \quad p_i = \nu_i \times^* I_i \nu_i - f^{ext}_i.
Body i‘s spatial Newton-Euler in body-i coords:
f_i - \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} {}^i X^*_c\, f_c = I_i a_i + p_i. \tag{NE-tree}
Articulated-body relation, proved by induction leaves → root. Claim:
f_i = I^A_i a_i + p^A_i. \tag{$\star$}
Base case (leaf). No children, so (NE-tree) gives f_\ell = I_\ell a_\ell + p_\ell, hence I^A_\ell = I_\ell,\ p^A_\ell = p_\ell.
Inductive step. Assume (\star) for each child c. From Pass 1, a_c = {}^c X_i\, a_i + S_c \ddot q_c + c_c =: a'_c + S_c \ddot q_c. Substitute into f_c = I^A_c a_c + p^A_c:
f_c = I^A_c a'_c + U_c \ddot q_c + p^A_c, \quad U_c := I^A_c S_c.
Apply joint constraint \tau_c = S_c^\top f_c and use symmetry of I^A_c (so S_c^\top I^A_c = U_c^\top):
\ddot q_c = D_c^{-1}\big(u_c - U_c^\top a'_c\big), \quad D_c := S_c^\top U_c, \quad u_c := \tau_c - S_c^\top p^A_c.
Back-substitute to eliminate \ddot q_c from f_c:
f_c = \tilde I^A_c\, a'_c + U_c D_c^{-1} u_c + p^A_c, \quad \tilde I^A_c := I^A_c - U_c D_c^{-1} U_c^\top.
The -U_c D_c^{-1} U_c^\top term projects the free joint axis S_c out of I^A_c: on the direction the joint accommodates, no inertia is felt. Sum the children into (NE-tree) and identify with (\star) to read off the recurrences:
I^A_i = I_i + \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} {}^i X^*_c\, \tilde I^A_c\, {}^c X_i \tag{R.1}
p^A_i = p_i + \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} {}^i X^*_c\, \Big( \tilde I^A_c\, c_c + U_c D_c^{-1} u_c + p^A_c \Big) \tag{R.2}
Induction closes. ∎
The three passes.

























PassDirectionComputes1root → leaves\nu_i, c_i, p_i; init I^A_i \leftarrow I_i,\ p^A_i \leftarrow p_i2leaves → rootU_i, D_i, u_i, \tilde I^A_i; accumulate I^A_{\lambda(i)}, p^A_{\lambda(i)} via (R.1, R.2)3root → leavesa'_i = {}^i X_{\lambda(i)}\, a_{\lambda(i)} + c_i; \ddot q_i = D_i^{-1}(u_i - U_i^\top a'_i); a_i = a'_i + S_i \ddot q_i
Gravity is folded in by setting a_0 = -g. Each pass touches every link with fixed-size 6×6 operations: O(n) total.
CRBA cousin. (R.1) mirrors the CRBA recurrence I^C_i = I_i + \sum_c {}^i X^*_c\, I^C_c\, {}^c X_i. The only structural change is I^C_c \to \tilde I^A_c: CRBA locks every joint (children contribute full composite inertias), ABA frees them (the joint axis is projected out). One substitution distinguishes lock-everything inverse dynamics from let-everything-move forward dynamics.
RNEA duality. The inverse-dynamics relation that drops out is \tau_i = D_i \ddot q_i + U_i^\top a'_i + S_i^\top p^A_i. RNEA computes this given \ddot q; ABA inverts it for \ddot q given \tau.
Why It Matters
ABA is what makes real-time forward dynamics on a humanoid tractable. For a Unitree G1 (n \approx 23 joints), CRBA-plus-Cholesky is dominated by an O(n^3) linear solve — order 10^4 flops just for the inversion — while ABA is O(n) with no global matrix assembly, on the order of 10^2 per-link 6×6 operations. This is the kernel that lets a Whole-Body-MPC stack integrate a 23-DOF model at 1 kHz, that lets PPO/SAC training rollouts hit hundreds of thousands of env-steps per second on CPU, and that makes Pinocchio’s aba(model, data, q, v, tau) a hot inner loop rather than a bottleneck. The crossover where ABA beats CRBA-plus-Cholesky is n \gtrsim 9, well below any humanoid.
Pinocchio’s analytic derivatives — needed for DDP/iLQR-style trajectory optimization and for backprop through dynamics in differentiable simulation — differentiate through the three passes, which is only tractable because each pass is a local recurrence over fixed-size blocks. There is no M^{-1} Jacobian to invert.
The body-frame EOM derivation matters specifically because \nu \times^* I \nu is the most commonly bungled piece of multibody dynamics code. It is not a Lagrangian Coriolis term, it is a coordinate-transport correction; once you see it as the dual of R^\top \dot R = \hat\omega, the spatial form writes itself instead of degenerating into a stacked 3+3 mess that has to be re-derived every time the frame convention changes.
Limitations

Tree topology only. Closed loops (parallel five-bar legs, four-bar linkages, dual-arm grasping, a humanoid with both feet planted treated rigidly) break the parent-map recurrence. The standard fix — cut loops, run ABA on the spanning tree, project constraint forces via Lagrange multipliers — re-introduces an O(m^3) solve over m loop-closure constraints, eroding the O(n) win.
Floating-base inversion cost. For a 6-DOF floating base, D_0 is 6×6 and dominates the per-step constant factor. Most of the wall-clock budget on a humanoid sits in this single block, not in the joint chain.
Symmetry is exact-arithmetic-only. I^A_i is symmetric by induction (R.1 is a congruence sandwich, UD^{-1}U^\top is symmetric), but FP rounding breaks it across hundreds of recurrence updates. Production code symmetrizes explicitly each iteration.
No contact dynamics for free. ABA produces \ddot q for prescribed \tau and external wrenches at the link frames. Rigid unilateral contact (LCP with \lambda \geq 0, \phi \geq 0) sits outside ABA and is typically solved via a Delassus-matrix construction that calls ABA n_c times for n_c contact points.
Rigid bodies, joint actuators only. Reflected rotor inertia is a one-line patch (add \gamma_i^2 I_{\text{rotor},i} to D_i). Link flexibility, gear backlash, and series-elastic actuation are not.

Open Questions

Reverse-mode AD through ABA. Forward-mode AD is straightforward (push tangents through the three passes). Reverse mode requires reversing the topological order and the cross-pass data dependencies (Pass 3 reads I^A_i, U_i, D_i produced in Pass 2 from \nu_i, p_i produced in Pass 1). What’s the minimal checkpointing scheme, and how does AD-generated code for \partial \ddot q / \partial q, \partial \ddot q / \partial \tau compare to Pinocchio’s hand-derived analytic Jacobians on cost and accuracy?
Residual-learning injection point. Plausible hooks: per-link bias on p^A_i (additive correction, preserves O(n) structure and locality), per-joint multiplier on D_i^{-1} (multiplicative gain on response, also local but breaks linearity in \tau), or a whole-tree post-hoc \Delta \ddot q correction (loses interpretability and any structural prior). The per-link bias seems strictly dominant — worth verifying on a real sim-to-real gap dataset.
Centroidal-momentum fusion. Wensing & Orin (2016) compute A_G(q) in O(n) using the same articulated-inertia scaffolding. Is there a single fused sweep producing both \ddot q and A_G \dot q, or does the algebra force two distinct passes?
Floating-base D_0 structure. In the centroidal frame the 6×6 base inertia decouples into angular and linear blocks. Worth checking whether exploiting this drops the base-solve below its current ~200-flop cost on a humanoid.
Implementation sanity. Code ABA from scratch on a 3-link planar chain and verify \ddot q against M^{-1}(\tau - h) computed via CRBA + Cholesky (Exercise 4 of the deck). Worth doing once with naked spatial algebra, no Pinocchio, to internalize the recurrences.

Links

10-robotics/control/Friction-Cone-Discre.title: Friction-Cone Discretisation
10-robotics/control/Friction-Cone-Discre.content:
Summary
The Coulomb friction model defines a convex cone of admissible contact forces; solving contact-rich QPs requires discretising this cone into either a linear pyramid approximation or an exact second-order cone. The choice propagates upward: pyramid errors in per-contact cones corrupt the Contact Wrench Cone (CWC) and therefore every centroidal feasibility check built on it. The deck gives constraint matrices, error bounds, and the wrench-level inequalities for a rectangular foot.
Core Mechanism
Coulomb cone. A contact force \mathbf{f} = f_n \hat{n} + \mathbf{f}_t is physically admissible iff
f_n \geq 0, \quad \|\mathbf{f}_t\|_2 \leq \mu f_n
This is a 3-D Lorentz cone \mathcal{L}^3 = \{(\mathbf{x}, t) : \|\mathbf{x}\| \leq t\}. The complementarity condition f_n \cdot \text{gap} = 0 enforces unilateral contact.
Linearised pyramid (LP form). Approximate the friction disc with a K-sided inscribed polygon:
\mathbf{f}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \beta_i \mathbf{d}_i, \quad \beta_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_i \beta_i \leq \mu f_n
\mathbf{d}_i = \mu\!\left(\cos\tfrac{2\pi i}{K},\, \sin\tfrac{2\pi i}{K}\right)
Inscribed polygon under-approximates (conservative); circumscribed over-approximates (relaxed). Approximation error for the inscribed case:
\cos(\pi/K) - 1 \approx -\frac{\pi^2}{2K^2}
K=4: ~30% error. K=8: ~8%. K=16: ~2%.
SOCP form (exact). The exact constraint is
(\mathbf{f}_t,\, \mu f_n) \in \mathcal{L}^3
Solved by primal-dual interior-point in O(N^{1.5} \log(1/\epsilon)). Solvers: ECOS, Mosek, OSQP-SOC. Overhead vs. pyramid: 5–10× slower per solve, but no K-knob bias.
Contact Wrench Cone (CWC) — rectangular foot. For a foot with half-extents (\ell_x, \ell_y), the necessary and sufficient wrench inequalities at the sole frame (Caron 2015):
f_z \geq 0
|f_x|,\, |f_y| \leq \mu f_z
|\tau_x| \leq \ell_y f_z, \quad |\tau_y| \leq \ell_x f_z
|\tau_z| \leq \mu_\tau f_z, \quad \mu_\tau = \mu(\ell_x + \ell_y)
Construction: take the convex hull of the union of per-corner friction cones, then express in wrench coordinates.
Multi-contact centroidal cone. The reachable centroidal wrench is the Minkowski sum of per-contact cones transformed to the CoM frame:
\mathcal{W} = \bigoplus_i {}^c\mathbf{X}^*_i \mathcal{K}_i
If every \mathcal{K}_i is polyhedral (pyramid discretisation), \mathcal{W} is a finitely-generated polyhedral cone computable via pycddlib/cdd-lib.
LCP formulation (Stewart–Trinkle). Time-stepping with linearised friction yields a mixed LCP:
M(q_k)v_{k+1} = M(q_k)v_k + h\left[\tau - C(q,v) - g\right] + h J_n^\top f_n + h J_t^\top \beta
with complementarities 0 \leq f_n \perp \phi_n \geq 0, 0 \leq \beta \perp E^\top f_n + \cdot \geq 0, 0 \leq \lambda \perp \mu f_n - E^\top\beta \geq 0. Solvable by Lemke’s algorithm. MuJoCo replaces this with a smooth convex relaxation for differentiability.
Why It Matters
In centroidal MPC for a bipedal robot, the feasibility constraint at each timestep is “does a wrench exist in the CWC?” If your CWC was built from K=4 pyramids, the MPC will consider some physically achievable wrenches infeasible (inscribed) or allow some that violate friction (circumscribed). The error shows up as a controller that either falls unnecessarily on high-\mu surfaces or issues torque commands the WBC can’t satisfy — not as a solver error, but as silent constraint mismatch between planning and execution.
Limitations

Pyramid approximation error is fixed at construction; it doesn’t adapt to contact geometry or \mu estimates.
The yaw friction moment bound \mu_\tau = \mu(\ell_x + \ell_y) is tiny for narrow feet (e.g. G1 where \ell_y \ll \ell_x), making yaw nearly uncontrollable through ground contact alone.
Minkowski sum of two SOCs is generally not an SOC — multi-contact SOCP cannot be collapsed to a single cone, requiring either enumeration or LP outer approximation.
LCP with Lemke is polynomial in the number of solution modes but can be slow on hardware; MuJoCo’s relaxation sacrifices physical accuracy for speed and differentiability.
CWC derivation assumes fixed contact positions; during transitions (heel-to-toe roll) the CWC changes shape mid-timestep.

Open Questions

Does the centroidal MPC’s inscribed pyramid systematically under- or over-promise control authority relative to the WBC’s exact SOC? The bias direction depends on whether the pyramid is inscribed or circumscribed — quantify this for your specific foot geometry.
For narrow-foot platforms where \mu_\tau is negligible, what is the minimum stepping bandwidth needed to recover yaw authority that the foot can’t provide?
Can a NN learn to predict the active pyramid corner (active constraint set) faster than the QP solves? What does the label space look like — one-hot over K faces, or a signed-distance to each face?
Is there a principled way to adapt K online based on estimated \mu and required wrench accuracy?

Links

10-robotics/control/HZD-and-Virtual-Cons.title: HZD and Virtual Constraints
10-robotics/control/HZD-and-Virtual-Cons.content:
Summary
Hybrid Zero Dynamics (HZD) provides the first formal stability proofs for periodic bipedal walking by reducing the full hybrid system to a low-dimensional zero-dynamics manifold and checking stability of the resulting Poincaré return map. Virtual constraints are output functions that pin the robot’s configuration to a designer-chosen curve parametrised by a phase variable, enforced via input-output feedback linearisation. The approach is conceptually orthogonal to centroidal MPC: it sacrifices online replanning for provable orbital stability.
Core Mechanism
Hybrid dynamical system. State x = (q, \dot{q}) \in TQ. Between impacts:
\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u, \quad x \notin \mathcal{S}
At impact:
x^+ = \Delta(x^-), \quad x^- \in \mathcal{S}
The switching surface \mathcal{S} = \{x : h_{\text{swing}}(q) = 0,\; \dot{h}_{\text{swing}} < 0\}. The impact map \Delta is derived from a plastic collision conserving angular momentum about the new contact point; stance and swing roles swap.
Virtual constraints. Define output functions
h(q) = h_d(\theta(q)) - h_a(q)
where \theta(q) is a monotone phase variable (e.g. stance hip angle) and h_d(\theta) is a Bézier polynomial. Drive h \to 0 via IO feedback linearisation:
\ddot{h} = L_f^2 h + L_g L_f h \cdot u \implies u = (L_g L_f h)^{-1}(v - L_f^2 h)
v = -K_d \dot{h} - K_p h
This is a coordinate change that pins the robot to a 2-D submanifold of TQ parametrised by \theta.
Zero dynamics manifold.
\mathcal{Z} = \{(q, \dot{q}) : h(q) = 0,\; L_f h(q, \dot{q}) = 0\} \subset TQ
Once on \mathcal{Z}, the dynamics reduce to a single second-order ODE in \theta:
\mathcal{D}(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + \mathcal{C}(\theta, \dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} + \mathcal{G}(\theta) = 0
where \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{G} are the Lagrangian coefficients pulled back to \mathcal{Z}. This admits a first integral (energy on \mathcal{Z}):
E(\theta, \dot{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathcal{D}(\theta)\dot{\theta}^2 + V(\theta)
A periodic orbit exists iff a closed level curve of E in the (\theta, \dot{\theta}) plane is consistent with the impact map \Delta.
Poincaré map and stability. Take a section \mathcal{S} just after each impact. The return map P : \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{S} has a fixed point x^\star = P(x^\star) corresponding to the periodic orbit. Stability condition:
\text{all eigenvalues of } DP(x^\star) \text{ inside the unit circle}
On \mathcal{Z}, the restricted map is 1-D in \dot{\theta}, reducing to a single scalar eigenvalue check. Westervelt–Grizzle (2003): if the restricted map is stable and transverse dynamics converge to \mathcal{Z} exponentially fast enough, the full hybrid system has a stable periodic orbit.
Compass-gait example. Two-link planar walker, slope \alpha. Phase variable \theta = q_1 (stance angle). Virtual constraint h(q) = q_2 - h_d(\theta) with cubic Bézier h_d. Stability check:
|DP|_{\mathcal{Z}}| = \left(\dot{\theta}^+/\dot{\theta}^-\right)^2 < 1 \quad \text{at the fixed point}
Why It Matters
In centroidal MPC you get disturbance rejection for free — push recovery is just another QP query. What you give up is a formal guarantee that the gait is periodic and orbitally stable. HZD fills that gap: if you are deploying on hardware where a proof of stability matters (certification, safe autonomy) and the environment is structured (flat ground, known terrain), the Poincaré eigenvalue check is a concrete certificate that centroidal stacks cannot provide. Cassie and MABEL used HZD-derived gait libraries precisely for this reason.
Limitations

Virtual constraint design is per-robot and per-gait; transferring to a different morphology requires redesign.
IO linearisation is exact only if L_g L_f h is invertible — near torque saturation or at kinematic singularities this breaks, requiring a CBF-QP fallback.
HZD classically assumes point-foot or rigid-foot contact with an instantaneous impact; planar polygon feet (e.g. G1) complicate the impact map derivation without breaking the framework entirely, but require multi-domain extensions.
Push recovery requires adding a separate reflex layer or switching to MPC; it is not intrinsic to the HZD controller.
Gait library approach (Da et al. 2017) mitigates rigidity but the library must be pre-computed offline and the switching logic adds implementation complexity.

Open Questions

What is the formal relationship between the centroidal MPC’s linearisation manifold and HZD’s \mathcal{Z}? Both are reduced-order submanifolds of TQ — can one be embedded in the other?
Can virtual constraints be learned from motion-capture data rather than hand-designed? How does this compare to AMP/ASE which implicitly learn a reference manifold from demonstrations?
For a planar polygon foot (non-point contact), the impact map must account for the choice of pivot corner — does this produce a multi-valued \Delta that breaks the Poincaré analysis or just adds cases?
How does friction-cone discretisation enter the zero dynamics? Specifically, if the pyramid approximation over-promises friction, does the periodic orbit of the zero dynamics remain consistent with the true contact constraints?

Links

10-robotics/learning/Constrained-Policy-.title: Constrained Policy Optimization (CPO) and Safe RL
10-robotics/learning/Constrained-Policy-.content:
Summary
Reward-shaping penalties give no per-iteration safety guarantee and introduce an untunable scale hyperparameter. CMDPs formalize safety as hard budget constraints J_{c_i}(\pi) \leq d_i, and CPO solves each policy update as a trust-region QP that enforces those constraints while maintaining monotone reward improvement. The result is a policy update with O(\sqrt{\delta})-bounded expected constraint violation per iteration and a recovery rule for infeasible iterates.
Core Mechanism
CMDP formulation
\max_\pi J_r(\pi) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad J_{c_i}(\pi) := \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\!\left[\sum_t \gamma^t c_i(s_t, a_t)\right] \leq d_i, \quad \forall i
Surrogate + KL bound (the trust-region motor)
Performance-difference identity (Kakade–Langford):
J(\pi') - J(\pi) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^{\pi'}, a \sim \pi'}\!\left[A^\pi(s,a)\right]
Replace d^{\pi'} with d^\pi (importance-sampled) to get the computable surrogate:
L_\pi(\pi') = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi}\!\left[\frac{\pi'(a|s)}{\pi(a|s)} A^\pi(s,a)\right]
with bound J(\pi') \geq L_\pi(\pi') - C\,\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi}\!\left[\mathrm{KL}(\pi'\|\pi)(s)\right].
CPO update step (linearise objective and constraints, quadratic KL):
\theta_{k+1} = \arg\max_\theta\; g^\top(\theta - \theta_k)
\text{s.t.}\quad \tfrac{1}{2}(\theta-\theta_k)^\top F(\theta-\theta_k) \leq \delta
b_i^\top(\theta - \theta_k) + e_i \leq 0, \quad e_i := J_{c_i}(\theta_k) - d_i
where g = \nabla_\theta J_r, b_i = \nabla_\theta J_{c_i}, F = Fisher information matrix.
Stationarity \Rightarrow closed-form step:
\Delta\theta = \frac{1}{\nu} F^{-1}\!\left(g - \textstyle\sum_i \lambda_i b_i\right)
\nu, \{\lambda_i\} from a small dual QP (analytic for m=1). F^{-1}v computed via conjugate gradient — no matrix inversion.
Infeasibility recovery (when J_{c_i}(\pi_k) > d_i):
\Delta\theta^{\text{rec}} = -\sqrt{\frac{2\delta}{b_i^\top F^{-1} b_i}}\, F^{-1} b_i
i.e., the steepest KL-step that reduces the constraint. Theorem: violation bounded O(\sqrt{\delta}) per iteration.
GAE for advantage estimation (both reward and cost critics):
\hat{A}^{\mathrm{GAE}(\gamma,\lambda)}_t = \sum_{l=0}^\infty (\gamma\lambda)^l \delta_{t+l}, \quad \delta_t = r_t + \gamma V_\phi(s_{t+1}) - V_\phi(s_t)
PPO-Lagrangian (weaker, no per-iteration guarantee):
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \nabla_\theta J_r - \sum_i \lambda_i \nabla_\theta J_{c_i}, \quad \lambda_i \leftarrow [\lambda_i + \eta(J_{c_i} - d_i)]_+
Stooke 2020: replace scalar \eta with a PID controller on \lambda to suppress oscillation.
CBF safety filter (certificate view, orthogonal to RL):
u^\star = \arg\min_u \tfrac{1}{2}\|u - u_{\text{nom}}\|^2 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad L_f h(x) + L_g h(x) u + \alpha(h(x)) \geq 0
Discrete-time analogue: enforce h(x_{k+1}) \geq (1-\eta)h(x_k); becomes a QCQP when h is concave quadratic and dynamics are affine.
Why It Matters
On a humanoid (Unitree G1 class): torque limits, friction-cone margins, and fall avoidance are non-negotiable hard constraints, not “soft preferences.” Reward shaping with a penalty r - \beta c has no per-rollout guarantee — \beta is scale-dependent, tail violations are unbounded, and a single bad update can collapse the gait. CPO lets you train with J_c \leq d enforced in expectation at every gradient step, so the constraint budget is a tunable engineering parameter (e.g., d = 0.05 torque-saturation rate) rather than an implicit consequence of \beta. The CBF layer can then be stacked on top to handle per-step set-invariance (friction cone) without retraining.
Limitations

Linearisation error: g, b_i are first-order Taylor truncations of the true objective/constraint surfaces. The O(\sqrt{\delta}) bound degrades when curvature is high (dense contact transitions, discontinuous reward).
Expectation-level safety only: J_{c_i}(\pi) \leq d_i is an average. Rare catastrophic events (spike torques, stumble recovery) are not bounded in probability — CVaR or chance constraints would be needed for tail safety.
Fisher approximation cost: CG solve per update is O(n_\theta \cdot k_{\text{CG}}); expensive for large networks. PPO-Lagrangian is faster but weaker.
Stochastic policy requirement: CMDP optimal policy may be stochastic even when the unconstrained optimum is deterministic. Deterministic actor architectures (DDPG-style) are incompatible without modification.
No inter-sample guarantee for discrete CBF: the linearised discrete CBF is tight only at the prediction step; at 100 Hz actuation, inter-sample violation is non-negligible (moot at 1 kHz).

Open Questions

Residual-RL constraint placement: for a policy \pi = \pi_{\text{MPC}} + \delta\pi, constraining \delta\pi alone gives tighter formal bounds (MPC feasibility domain is known) but requires the MPC to be admissible at all times. Constraining total action is easier to implement but widens the feasible set.
CPO + CBF composition: CPO provides monotone improvement in expectation; CBF provides per-step set-invariance. The composition — run CPO offline, deploy with CBF filter online — is sound but the CBF distorts the action distribution the policy was trained under, creating a train/deploy mismatch. How large is that gap?
CVaR constraints in the CMDP: replacing J_{c_i} \leq d with a CVaR constraint changes the dual variable structure; the Lagrangian is no longer linear in the policy and the conjugate-gradient trick breaks. What’s the right surrogate?
Early termination vs. finite \lambda: ET = \lambda \to \infty kills gradient through the constraint manifold; Safety-Gym benchmarks show 3–5× slower learning. Is there a curriculum schedule \lambda(k) that recovers ET’s conservatism late in training while preserving CPO’s gradient flow early?

References

10-robotics/perception/Observability-of-.title: Observability of the Pseudo-Measurement and EKF Convergence
10-robotics/perception/Observability-of-.content:
Summary
The contact wrench pseudo-measurement \hat{W} = (J(q)^\top)^+ (M(q)\ddot{q} + h(q,\dot{q}) - S^\top \tau_{\text{cmd}}) loses rank at kinematic singularities, making certain force directions unobservable to the EKF. Standard observability theory (linear Gramian, nonlinear Lie-derivative rank condition) provides the diagnostic; the fix is a damped pseudo-inverse plus Q-projection in the Riccati update to stop noise from inflating along unobservable directions. Persistence of excitation is the system-identification analogue: wrench identification requires diverse contact accelerations spanning the full generalised velocity space.
Core Mechanism
Linear observability. System \dot{x} = Ax + Bu, y = Cx. The observability matrix:
\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{nm \times n}
(A, C) is observable iff \text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n. Equivalent PBH test: \text{rank}\begin{bmatrix} \lambda I - A \\ C \end{bmatrix} = n for every eigenvalue \lambda. Unobservable subspace = \ker \mathcal{O}.
For LTV systems, replace with the observability Gramian:
W_O(t_0, t_1) = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} \Phi(t)^\top C^\top C \Phi(t)\, dt
Uniform complete observability \iff W_O uniformly positive definite.
Nonlinear observability (Hermann–Krener 1977). System \dot{x} = f(x,u), y = h(x). Lie derivatives:
L_f h(x) = \nabla h(x)^\top f(x,u), \quad L_f^k h \text{ recursively}
Observation space \mathcal{O}(x) = \text{span}\{dh,\, dL_f h,\, dL_f^2 h,\, \ldots\}. Local weak observability at x_0: \dim \mathcal{O}(x_0) = n.
Empirical observability Gramian (Krener–Ide 2009) for numerical evaluation:
W^{\text{emp}}_{ij} = \int_0^T \frac{(y_i^+(t) - y_i^-(t))(y_j^+(t) - y_j^-(t))}{(2\epsilon)^2}\, dt
where y^\pm are trajectories from x_0 \pm \epsilon e_{i,j}. Converges to the analytical Gramian as \epsilon \to 0.
Pseudo-measurement rank deficiency. The contact wrench estimate:
\hat{W} = (J(q)^\top)^+\!\left(M(q)\ddot{q} + h(q,\dot{q}) - S^\top \tau_{\text{cmd}}\right)
J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{6n_c \times n_v}. When J is rank-deficient, null directions of J^\top correspond to internal forces (e.g. leg pushing against pelvis) invisible to \ddot{q}. Key singularities on G1:

Knee fully extended → stance Jacobian rank drops by 1.
Both feet flat and parallel → yaw moment collapses.
Single-support with hip axis yaw-aligned with ankle axis.

Fix: damped pseudo-inverse with condition-number-adapted regularisation:
(J J^\top + \alpha I)^{-1} J, \quad \alpha = \alpha(\text{cond}(J))
EKF convergence under partial observability. Riccati update:
P_{k+1} = (I - K_{k+1} C_{k+1}) F_k P_k F_k^\top + Q
If (F, C) is uniformly completely observable, P_k \to P_\infty (bounded). Loss of observability in some directions \Rightarrow P_k unbounded along the unobservable subspace unless Q has no support there. Fix (Krener 2003): project Q onto the observable subspace — inject process noise only where the measurement can see it:
Q_{\text{proj}} = \Pi_{\text{obs}}\, Q\, \Pi_{\text{obs}}^\top, \quad \Pi_{\text{obs}} = \text{row}(C_k)\text{ projector}
Persistence of excitation (PE). For the adaptive identifier \dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \phi(t) e(t), convergence \hat{\theta} \to \theta^\star requires:
\exists\, \alpha > 0,\, T > 0 \text{ s.t. } \int_t^{t+T} \phi\phi^\top\, ds \succeq \alpha I \quad \forall t
A single push recovery event fails PE: the regressor \phi has rank 1 for a \sim0.1 s window. Diverse contact accelerations spanning \mathbb{R}^{n_v} are required. Diagnostic: monitor the smallest eigenvalue of \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \phi\phi^\top\, dt — below \sim 10^{-3} signals rank deficiency.
CoM observability from encoders only. Output y = q_{\text{joint}}; state includes q_{\text{base}} \in SE(3). CoM c = \sum_i m_i r_i(q)/M depends on q_{\text{base}} which is invisible to encoders — global SE(3) ambiguity. With IMU: q^{\text{rot}}_{\text{base}} observable up to yaw drift. With contact constraint (one foot flat): base position pinned; CoM fully observable. Double-support transition is the most observable moment; flight phase is the worst.
Why It Matters
In a state estimator for a legged robot, the EKF is fed a wrench pseudo-measurement derived from the contact Jacobian. At the exact instant the robot straightens its knee to transition between crouching and standing, J loses rank and the measurement noise in the yaw-moment channel becomes infinite — but the Riccati equation doesn’t know that unless you detect it. Without Q-projection, the EKF injects noise into the yaw moment state, the downstream MPC sees spurious disturbances, and the robot makes unnecessary corrective steps. Detecting the rank drop and projecting Q eliminates this without changing the estimator architecture.
Limitations

The empirical observability Gramian requires running perturbed trajectories, which is expensive on hardware and infeasible in real-time — it is a design-time diagnostic, not an online tool.
Damped pseudo-inverse with fixed \alpha trades rank-deficiency robustness for bias in the wrench estimate; adapting \alpha to the condition number requires online singular value computation.
Q-projection requires detecting rank-deficient C_k at every EKF step; numerical rank determination has tolerance sensitivity near singularities.
PE for wrench identification requires deliberate excitation (chirp + perturbations); closed-loop walking alone is insufficient and combining datasets introduces domain mismatch.
Linear EKF observability analysis around \hat{x}_k is only locally valid; the nonlinear Lie-derivative rank condition is the correct global check but harder to compute online.

Open Questions

Can the controller deliberately excite observability by injecting a small probing signal during quiet phases (dual control / Bar-Shalom)? The cost is non-zero perturbation of the nominal trajectory; quantify the trade-off as a constrained optimisation over probing amplitude.
If \hat{W} is unobservable in one direction, a learned actuator network trained on \hat{W} will see zero gradient in that direction during training — it will not generalise to perturbations along the unobservable subspace. This is a principled failure mode, not a generalisation gap.
Is the empirical observability Gramian related to the neural tangent kernel of a network trained on the same trajectories? Both measure how output variation relates to parameter/state perturbation — the connection may yield a learning-based observability diagnostic.
What is the simplest 1-D worked example of dual control (Bar-Shalom)? A scalar system with unknown gain and a cost that penalises both tracking error and estimation variance would make the exploration-exploitation trade-off explicit.

Links
EKF contact-jacobian pseudo-measurement persistence-of-excitation observability-gramian centroidal-mpc WBC

20-embedded/compilers/Register-Allocatio.title: Register Allocation and Spilling
20-embedded/compilers/Register-Allocatio.content:
Summary
After instruction selection, the compiler must map an unbounded set of SSA virtual registers to a fixed physical register file plus stack. The canonical formulation is graph k-coloring on the interference graph, which is NP-complete for k \geq 3, so production compilers use heuristic allocators (Chaitin-Briggs, linear scan, greedy, PBQP). When coloring fails, the allocator picks a vreg to spill to memory according to a cost heuristic and retries.

Core Mechanism
Setup. Liveness analysis produces a live range [s_v, e_v] for each vreg v. Two vregs interfere if their ranges overlap. The interference graph G = (V, E) has V = {\text{vregs}} and E = {(u,v) : \text{ranges overlap}}. With k physical registers, the allocation problem is:
\min_{S \subseteq V} \sum_{v \in S} c(v) \quad \text{s.t. } G[V \setminus S] \text{ is } k\text{-colorable}
where c(v) is the spill cost of v and S is the spill set. Both the feasibility check (k-colorability) and the objective are NP-hard, so all practical work approximates simultaneously.
Chaitin-Briggs (graph coloring). Iterate until colored:

Simplify: while \exists v with \deg(v) < k, push v onto stack, remove from G. The simplification lemma: a node with fewer than k neighbors is always colorable after its neighbors are.
Potential spill: if no such v exists, pick spill candidate v^* = \arg\min_v c(v), remove it.
Select: pop stack, greedily assign a color \notin colors of colored neighbors.
Rewrite: if any node was marked for spill, insert loads/stores at uses/defs, re-run liveness, restart.

Chaitin’s spill cost heuristic:
c(v) = \frac{(|\text{defs}(v)| + |\text{uses}(v)|) \cdot w_{\text{loop}}(v)}{\deg_G(v)}
High degree → spilling relieves more pressure; low use count → cheap to reload.
Linear scan. Sort live intervals by start point. Maintain active set A of currently-allocated intervals. For each new interval i: expire any j \in A with e_j < s_i (free its reg); if |A| < k assign a free reg; else spill the interval in A \cup {i} with latest end point. O(n \log n) vs. Chaitin’s O(n^2), at the cost of over-approximating interference (two intervals that overlap on the timeline may not actually interfere in the SSA dataflow sense).
Iterated Register Coalescing (George-Appel). Extends Chaitin-Briggs with conservative move elimination. Coalesce a, b across mov a, b iff one of:

George: every neighbor t of a with \deg(t) \geq k already interferes with b.
Briggs: the merged node ab has fewer than k neighbors of degree \geq k.

These tests guarantee coalescing never makes a colorable graph uncolorable.
PBQP (Partitioned Boolean Quadratic Problem). Formulates allocation as minimizing \sum_v C_v(x_v) + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} C_{uv}(x_u, x_v) where x_v is v‘s register assignment, C_v is a per-node cost vector (preferences, spill cost), and C_{uv} is an interference penalty matrix. Solved via belief propagation. Still NP-hard but tractable for realistic graph sizes.
Why It Matters
This is the pass that decides, for every variable you write, whether it lives in a register or thrashes through L1 — and it runs every time anything is compiled. Concretely:

JIT compilers (V8, HotSpot, LLVM -O0) use linear scan because compile time is on the critical path of execution; the spill penalty is acceptable.
AOT optimizing builds (-O2/-O3) use greedy or IRC because the user pays compile time once and runtime forever.
GPU compilers treat register allocation as an occupancy problem: a kernel using too many registers reduces the number of concurrent warps, so the spill/occupancy tradeoff is more important than spill count alone.
Embedded targets with k = 8 or fewer GPRs (AVR, MSP430) make spill decisions visible in benchmark numbers — a missed coalesce in a tight loop can double cycle count.

Limitations

All practical allocators are heuristic; for adversarial graphs they spill more than the optimum. The gap rarely matters but is unbounded in principle.
Spill cost models treat all memory accesses identically — they don’t model L1 vs. L2 reload, store-to-load forwarding, or the fact that a spilled vreg in a hot loop may be the actual bottleneck even with low “cost.”
Linear scan’s interval abstraction is strictly weaker than the interference graph: it can force spills that graph coloring would avoid. The damage is small on straight-line code, larger on irregular control flow.
Phase ordering: register allocation and instruction scheduling each constrain the other. Schedule-first inflates live ranges; allocate-first removes scheduling freedom. Most compilers pick one and live with it.
Aggressive coalescing without George/Briggs tests increases degree and causes spills (the original Chaitin bug).
SSA destruction (where the allocator leaves SSA form) introduces parallel copies that interact subtly with coalescing.

Open Questions

How much do learned spill-cost predictors (cf. MLGO-style approaches) actually move the needle on standard benchmarks vs. the Chaitin heuristic? The function looks crude but is hard to beat.
For GPU codegen, is there a principled way to fold occupancy targets into the allocator’s objective rather than running allocation under a hard register budget set by an outer loop?
PBQP is “real” optimization but rarely shipped — what’s the actual compile-time cost on a kernel-scale function, and where does it win over greedy in practice?
Live-range splitting (breaking a long-lived vreg into pieces, each separately allocatable) is where modern allocators get most of their wins over Chaitin-Briggs. Implementing it well is the actual engineering problem.

Links
ssa-form liveness-analysis graph-coloring instruction-scheduling llvm-codegen-pipeline pbqp

20-embedded/compilers/chaitin-register-a.title: Register Allocation & Spilling via Graph Coloring
20-embedded/compilers/chaitin-register-a.content:
Summary
Mapping unbounded symbolic registers to k physical registers is equivalent to k-coloring an interference graph; when k-coloring fails, nodes must be spilled to memory. Chaitin extends the coloring algorithm to make spill decisions inside the same graph-reduction loop, guided by a cost/degree heuristic. The result replaces ad hoc spill heuristics with a unified, principled algorithm that produces better code in less compile time.
Core Mechanism
Interference graph construction. Two symbolic registers r_i, r_j interfere iff one is live at a definition point of the other. Build G = (V, E) where V = symbolic registers, E = interference pairs. Represented as: symmetric |V| \times |V| bit matrix (O(1) adjacency query) + per-node adjacency vector (O(deg) sequential scan). Two-pass: first pass computes degrees; second fills adjacency vectors.
Coalescing (subsumption). For each copy instruction r_i \leftarrow r_j: if {r_i, r_j} \notin E, merge nodes, eliminating the copy. Iterate until fixpoint (2–3 passes in practice).
Coloring via simplification. Chaitin’s algorithm for k-coloring G:

If \exists node v with \deg(v) < k: remove v, recurse on G' = G \setminus {v}, reinsert v, assign any color not used by neighbors. Correctness: v has < k neighbors so a free color always exists.
If all nodes have \deg \geq k: algorithm is blocked — must spill.

Spill selection when blocked. Pick node v^* to spill:
v^* = \arg\min_{v \in V} \frac{\text{cost}(v)}{\deg(v)}
where
\text{cost}(v) = \sum_{\text{def/use sites } s \text{ of } v} \text{freq}(s)
with \text{freq}(s) \approx 10^{\text{loop_depth}(s)} (loop nesting approximation). Special cases: recomputable values and copy sources/targets can have cost 0 or negative. Local-only values with no intervening deaths get \text{cost} = \infty (spilling them cannot free a color).
Spill insertion and iteration. Spilling v replaces it with short-lived temporaries: a fresh r_{\text{tmp}} loaded immediately before each use, stored immediately after each definition. Rebuild G, reattempt coloring. Repeat until a k-coloring succeeds (converges rapidly; successive graphs shrink).
Full pipeline:
build_graph → coalesce → color_graph
    └─ blocked? → estimate_costs → decide_spills
                → insert_spill_code → rebuild_graph → retry

Why It Matters
This lets you generate near-hand-coded register schedules from a high-level language compiler in a target with many registers (e.g., 32 GPRs on the IBM 801, or modern RISC-V/ARM with 31 integer registers). Previously, spill decisions were made by ad hoc local heuristics after coloring failed, producing redundant load/store pairs and burning compile time. The graph-integrated approach amortizes the spill decision into the same O(|V| + |E|) simplification pass — the bottleneck is building the graph the first time; all retries operate on strictly smaller graphs.
Concrete scenario: a hot inner loop with 40 live values competing for 32 registers. The old approach might spill 8 values naively; Chaitin’s heuristic spills the ones cheapest to reload (e.g., loop-invariant constants that can be recomputed), minimizing added load/store traffic inside the loop body.
Limitations

Spill cost is approximate. Loop depth proxy (\times 10 per level) is a heuristic, not a profile. Mispredicts for irregular control flow (e.g., cold branches inside hot loops).
Graph rebuild after spill is not exact. Spilling a globally-live value should produce multiple short-lived nodes immediately; instead the graph is rebuilt from scratch, requiring iteration. In pathological cases this loop can require many passes.
NP-completeness evaded, not solved. The simplification heuristic is not guaranteed to find the minimum-spill solution; it is a greedy approximation. Optimal k-coloring is NP-complete.
Coalescing is conservative. Only non-interfering copies are coalesced; no attempt is made to break interferences to enable coalescing (cf. later work on iterated coalescing by George & Appel, 1996).
Memory pressure. The full |V|^2 bit matrix must fit in core during allocation — noted explicitly as a practical constraint.

Open Questions

How does the cost heuristic interact with profile-guided optimization? Replacing the loop-depth proxy with actual branch frequencies is straightforward in principle but requires PGO infrastructure.
What is the convergence bound on the spill-rebuild loop? The paper claims rapid convergence empirically but gives no formal bound.
George & Appel (1996) revisit coalescing — how much does conservative coalescing here leave on the table in copy-heavy IR (e.g., SSA with many φ-copies)?
Does the \text{cost}/\deg heuristic still perform well on architectures with register classes (FP vs. integer, vector), where the interference graph is partitioned?
Can the bit-matrix representation be replaced with a sparse structure (e.g., CSR) without losing the O(1) adjacency query, to relieve the memory constraint?

Links
graph-coloring liveness-analysis ssa-form compiler-intermediate-representation register-pressure loop-optimization

20-embedded/rtos/Real-Time-Digital-Twin-.title: Real-Time Digital Twin Synchronisation
20-embedded/rtos/Real-Time-Digital-Twin-.content:
Summary
A digital twin (DT) running alongside a real-time controller is only as useful as the tightness of its synchronisation with the physical robot; non-RT kernels and default DDS configurations introduce jitter that silently lags the DT state by one or more control periods. The deck decomposes the end-to-end timing budget, identifies the dominant jitter sources, and gives the interpolation options for bridging sensor-rate gaps inside faster control loops. The goal is to treat DT synchronisation as a first-class real-time design problem with explicit budgets.
Core Mechanism
Latency budget. End-to-end sensor → controller → actuator decomposition:
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sensor}} + T_{\text{DDS,pub}} + T_{\text{wire}} + T_{\text{DDS,sub}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{DDS,act}} + T_{\text{actuator}}
Typical values: T_{\text{sensor}} \approx 1–5 ms, DDS overhead \approx 1 ms total (Cyclone DDS on Ubuntu 22.04), T_{\text{compute}} \approx 2 ms. Control bandwidth is upper-bounded by \omega_{\text{ctrl}} \lesssim 1/(10\, T_{\text{total}}). For 1 kHz control, a 1 ms total budget is infeasible on a stock kernel.
DDS QoS knobs (ROS 2). The key per-pub/sub settings:

Reliability: RELIABLE (TCP-like retransmit) vs. BEST_EFFORT (UDP-like drop).
Durability: VOLATILE vs. TRANSIENT_LOCAL (late-joiner replay).
History: KEEP_LAST(N) depth tunes the queue.
Deadline / Lifespan: per-sample temporal contracts.

For high-rate sensor streams: BEST_EFFORT + KEEP_LAST(1) — never stall waiting for a retransmit. For low-rate config: RELIABLE + TRANSIENT_LOCAL.
Jitter mitigations.

Kernel preemption: install PREEMPT_RT or Xenomai; stock kernels can preempt threads for tens of microseconds.
CPU scheduling: pin RT threads with SCHED_FIFO; isolate cores via isolcpus boot argument.
Memory: use rclcpp::executors::StaticSingleThreadedExecutor + pre-allocated buffers to avoid malloc inside the loop.
Logging: use lock-free queues + a low-priority logger thread; never do file I/O on the RT thread.
DDS discovery: lock topology in production with static endpoint discovery; multicast discovery generates bursts.

Mid-cycle interpolation. Telemetry arrives at f_{\text{tel}} (~250 Hz); DT runs at f_{\text{ctrl}} (1 kHz). Options between samples:






























MethodDelayErrorZero-order holdnonebiased low, O(T_{\text{tel}})Linear interpolation1/f_{\text{tel}} look-aheadlower mean errorCubic Hermite1/f_{\text{tel}} look-aheadmatches velocity at knotsKalman predictnonevariance grows as \Sigma_{k+1} = F\Sigma_k F^\top + Q
The Kalman predict step between sensor arrivals:
\hat{x}_{t+\Delta} = F\hat{x}_t + Bu_t, \quad \Sigma_{t+\Delta} = F\Sigma_t F^\top + Q\Delta t
No look-ahead delay; uncertainty inflates monotonically between measurements. For a constant-velocity model, \Sigma grows linearly in the inter-sample interval.
Clock discipline. Use CLOCK_MONOTONIC for periodic schedulers to avoid NTP jumps. Synchronise PC and robot clocks with PTP (IEEE 1588) for sub-microsecond alignment. Log timestamps at the source, not at receive time — DDS queueing adds an invisible offset that corrupts latency estimates.
Phase margin constraint. For a control loop with bandwidth \omega_c and required phase margin \phi_m, the maximum tolerable round-trip delay T_d satisfies:
\omega_c T_d \leq \frac{\pi - \phi_m}{1} \quad \text{(rad)}
For \omega_c = 10 Hz and \phi_m = 60° = \pi/3:
T_d \leq \frac{\pi - \pi/3}{2\pi \cdot 10} = \frac{2\pi/3}{20\pi} \approx 33\text{ ms}
Zero-order hold introduces an equivalent phase lag of \phi(\omega) = -\omega T/2 at frequency \omega, where T is the sample period.
Why It Matters
A centroidal DT running at 1 kHz on top of a 250 Hz sensor stream — a common configuration on G1-class hardware — will silently feed stale state to the MPC unless interpolation and jitter are explicitly managed. The failure mode is not a crash; it is a CoM trajectory estimate that flickers or lags by 4–8 ms, causing the MPC to issue torque commands based on a state that is no longer true. On a non-RT kernel the lag is non-deterministic, making it impossible to compensate with a fixed predictor.
Limitations

PREEMPT_RT reduces worst-case jitter to ~50 µs but does not eliminate it; hard 1 µs guarantees require dedicated RTOS or FPGA-based timing.
Kalman predict requires an accurate process model; for high-dimensional robot state, the linear constant-velocity assumption breaks during dynamic manoeuvres, causing variance underestimation.
PTP requires hardware timestamping support; software PTP degrades to ~10 µs accuracy, not sub-microsecond.
BEST_EFFORT + KEEP_LAST(1) can silently drop frames under CPU load; message-loss rate must be monitored explicitly.
Static endpoint discovery locks the DDS topology — incompatible with ad-hoc system reconfiguration during development.

Open Questions

Should the DT publish at the control rate (one tick drives everything) or at the sensor rate (no extrapolation)? The control-rate option requires on-board interpolation but decouples consumers; the sensor-rate option avoids extrapolation error but forces consumers to interpolate independently.
For a 200 Hz MPC on top of a 1 kHz DT, where does Kalman predict help vs. hurt? It helps when the sensor rate is low relative to MPC rate; it hurts when the process model is wrong and uncertainty is underestimated.
How do you measure ground-truth latency between robot and DT without trusting the DT’s own timestamps? Hardware triggering (GPIO pulse on both clocks, measure offset optically or via oscilloscope) is the cleanest method.
Can a residual NN compensate for DT lag conditioned on the most recent sensor burst? Risk: the NN learns to correct for the specific jitter profile of the training hardware; distributional shift in jitter at deployment causes the correction to add noise rather than remove it.

Links
ROS2-DDS real-time-linux kalman-filter centroidal-mpc digital-twin MPC-timing

30-math/optimization/Active-Set-Sensitiv.title: Active-Set Sensitivity in Differentiable MPC
30-math/optimization/Active-Set-Sensitiv.content:
Summary
Differentiating through a parametric QP (as in OptNet / MPC.pytorch) requires \partial z^\star / \partial \theta, which exists and is C^1 only while the active set \mathcal{A}(\theta) is constant. The derivation applies the implicit function theorem to the reduced KKT system; the resulting linear solve gives the Jacobian without re-solving the QP. At active-set boundaries the gradient is set-valued (Clarke subdifferential), and any autodiff library silently picks one element. Empirically, two failure modes show up in even the smallest bilevel toy problem: (i) silent disagreement with finite differences at degenerate KKT points, and (ii) outer-loop convergence to a projected — not global — optimum when the inner Jacobian becomes rank-deficient on an active manifold.
Core Mechanism
Parametric QP.
z^\star(\theta) = \arg\min_z \tfrac{1}{2} z^\top Q(\theta) z + q(\theta)^\top z \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A(\theta)z = b(\theta),\; G(\theta)z \le h(\theta)
KKT residual (active constraints only, under strict complementarity).
F(z, \nu, \lambda_\mathcal{A};\, \theta) = \begin{bmatrix} Qz + q + A^\top\nu + G_\mathcal{A}^\top\lambda_\mathcal{A} \\ Az - b \\ G_\mathcal{A}z - h_\mathcal{A} \end{bmatrix} = 0
IFT application. Since F(z^\star, \nu^\star, \lambda^\star_\mathcal{A}; \theta_0) = 0, the IFT gives
\frac{\partial(z^\star, \nu^\star, \lambda^\star_\mathcal{A})}{\partial \theta} = -K^{-1} \frac{\partial F}{\partial \theta}, \qquad K = \begin{bmatrix} Q & A^\top & G_\mathcal{A}^\top \\ A & 0 & 0 \\ G_\mathcal{A} & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
K is invertible iff LICQ holds ([A;\, G_\mathcal{A}] has full row rank — guarantees unique multipliers) and SOSC holds (d^\top Q d > 0 for all d in the critical cone — guarantees z^\star is an isolated minimiser). Proof sketch: Kv = 0 \Rightarrow d_z lies in the critical cone \xrightarrow{\text{SOSC}} d_z = 0 \xrightarrow{\text{LICQ}} d_\nu = d_\lambda = 0.
Coding loop (challenge.py). For the outer bilevel problem \min_q \tfrac{1}{2}\|z^\star(q) - z_\text{tgt}\|^2:

Solve inner QP → get z^\star, \lambda^\star.
Detect \mathcal{A} = \{i : \lambda_i^\star > \epsilon\} (used \epsilon = 10^{-6}).
Assemble K, solve K[\partial z^\star;\, \partial\lambda_\mathcal{A}]^\top = -[I;\,0] for \partial z^\star / \partial q.
Outer gradient: \nabla_q \ell = (\partial z^\star/\partial q)^\top (z^\star - z_\text{tgt}).
Update q \leftarrow q - \eta\,\nabla_q \ell (used \eta = 0.1, 200 iterations).

At boundaries — Clarke subdifferential. For locally Lipschitz z^\star (Robinson’s theorem guarantees this; Rademacher’s theorem then ensures the limit Jacobians in the definition below exist a.e., so the set is non-empty):
\partial^C z^\star(\theta) = \operatorname{conv}\!\left\{\lim_k \nabla z^\star(\theta_k) : \theta_k \to \theta,\; z^\star \text{ differentiable at } \theta_k\right\}
Box example: z^\star(\theta) = \operatorname{clip}(\theta,-1,1), \partial^C z^\star(1) = [0, 1]. Autodiff picks one point silently.
Results
Ran challenge.py (problem: z \in \mathbb{R}^2, Q = I, constraints z_1+z_2\le 1, z \ge 0, target z_\text{tgt} = (0.3, 0.6)). Three regimes tested.
(a) Interior, well-behaved active region — q = (-1, -1). Unconstrained optimum -q = (1, 1) violates z_1+z_2\le 1, so constraint 0 binds: \mathcal{A} = \{0\}, z^\star = (0.5, 0.5). Analytic and finite-difference Jacobians agree to \mathbf{3 \times 10^{-12}}:
\partial z^\star/\partial q = \begin{bmatrix}-0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -0.5\end{bmatrix}
This is the IFT working exactly as advertised — and notice the determinant is zero (foreshadowing the rank-deficiency result below).
(b) Degenerate KKT, silent failure — q = (-0.5, -0.5). Unconstrained optimum z^\star = (0.5, 0.5) lies exactly on z_1+z_2 = 1, but the solver returns \lambda = 0. Strict complementarity fails. With the active-set threshold \epsilon = 10^{-6}, the code classifies this as \mathcal{A} = \emptyset and returns analytic Jacobian -I. Central differences return
\begin{bmatrix}-0.75 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & -0.75\end{bmatrix} = \tfrac{1}{2}(-I) + \tfrac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}-0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -0.5\end{bmatrix}
— exactly the mean of the two adjacent regime Jacobians, a Clarke subgradient element. The disagreement is O(1), not O(\epsilon); no NaN, no warning, no convergence failure. The two-dimensional analogue of the box example from the deck, observed in 30 lines of Python.
(c) Rank-deficient Jacobian, stuck optimisation — q_0 = (-1, -2). Initialised on the active manifold z_1+z_2=1. Active set stays \{0\} for all 200 iterations. Final state:

q_\text{final} = (-1.35, -1.65), z^\star_\text{final} = (0.35, 0.65)
final loss = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} (not zero), target z_\text{tgt} = (0.3, 0.6)

The outer Jacobian \partial z^\star/\partial q has rank 1 on this active set (determinant zero, null space spanned by (1, 1) — the direction that would deactivate the constraint). Gradient descent literally cannot see that direction. The optimisation converges to the projection of z_\text{tgt} onto the active manifold \{z : z_1+z_2=1\}, not to z_\text{tgt} itself. A correct solution requires either a larger step (overshoots the constraint), regularisation that pushes q into a different regime, or an interior-point smoothing of the inner problem.
Why It Matters
In humanoid centroidal MPC, contact forces hit friction-cone boundaries and joint torques saturate mid-rollout. The active set \mathcal{A} changes exactly then. Any system-identification or cost-learning loop that backprops through the MPC (e.g., fitting Q, q from motion capture) will receive a spurious gradient at those transitions — not NaN, just wrong. Knowing which iteration the transition occurred (visible from the dual \lambda^\star going to zero) tells you whether to trust the analytic Jacobian or fall back to finite differences for that step.
The result (c) above is the more insidious failure mode: when the outer loop runs entirely inside one active regime, the gradient is perfectly correct locally but the global problem is unsolvable by gradient descent. In MPC training this looks like a training curve that plateaus mid-loss with no sign of trouble.
Separately: the sensitivity warm-start trick z^\star_{k+1} \approx z^\star_k + (\partial z^\star/\partial\theta)\Delta\theta (Diehl & Bock) replaces a full QP solve with one linear solve — practical for MPC at \geq 1 kHz.
Limitations

Active-set changes invalidate the Jacobian. The IFT is local; K^{-1} is only valid while \mathcal{A} is constant. Transitions happen on a measure-zero set of \theta, but they concentrate exactly in the dynamically interesting regimes (contact events, saturation).
Degenerate KKT (LICQ failure). Redundant active constraints (e.g., both feet + hand fully constraining the CoM) make [A;\,G_\mathcal{A}] rank-deficient; K is singular. Pirnay et al. 2012 handle this for IPOPT; the analogous SOCP result (friction cones) is an open question.
Strict complementarity assumption. At \lambda^\star_i = 0 with G_i z^\star = h_i (degenerate active constraint), the inactive-multiplier argument breaks down and \partial_\theta \lambda^\star_i \ne 0 in general. Empirically observed in result (b) above — the analytic Jacobian and FD disagree by O(1) with no warning.
Rank-deficient outer Jacobian on active sets. When constraints bind, \partial z^\star/\partial \theta lives in the tangent space of the constraint manifold (rank = n - |\mathcal{A}|). Outer gradient descent cannot move z^\star off the active set; bilevel loops can converge to manifold-projected, not globally optimal, solutions. Empirically observed in result (c). Mitigation: regularise \theta to stay away from boundaries, use multi-start, or smooth the inner problem (interior-point).
Smoothing bias. Log-barrier workarounds (OptNet’s approach) give C^\infty gradients but introduce bias O(1/t) between the barrier solution z_t and the true z^\star.

Open Questions

For the centroidal MPC push-recovery case: which constraint (|f_x| \le \mu f_z vs. joint torque limit) is active most often? Could you precompute a stratified K^{-1} per expected active-set “patch” and cache it?
Regularising dual variables to avoid transitions (penalise \|\lambda\| in the outer loss) reduces gradient variance in PPO but costs optimality — what is the Pareto frontier empirically?
Does the SOCP friction cone admit the same parametric sensitivity theory as the LP/QP case? (Pirnay et al. 2012 covers NLP; SOCP structure may allow a cleaner closed form via cone projection Jacobians.)
The rank-deficiency stuck-optimum failure in result (c) suggests a diagnostic: monitor \operatorname{rank}(\partial z^\star/\partial\theta) during training and alert when it drops. Is this a known technique in the differentiable-optimisation literature, or worth writing up?

30-math/optimization/Physics-Informed-Ne.title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks — Forward and Inverse Problems {WIP}
30-math/optimization/Physics-Informed-Ne.content:
Summary
PINNs encode PDE structure directly into a neural network’s loss by adding a collocation residual \text{MSE}_f alongside the data-fit term \text{MSE}_u.
Automatic differentiation (c.f. automatic-differentiation) through the network’s inputs provides the required spatial/temporal derivatives.
From here, there are two problems we can deal with: in the forward problem, known physical parameters \lambda constrain the solution manifold; in the inverse problem, \lambda (contact stiffness, viscosity, actuator delay) is optimised together with the network weights - this is the practical use case for system identification on hardware where the URDF is wrong. Identifiability is governed by the rank of a Fisher information matrix \mathbf{F} — a direct analogue of the rank-deficient outer Jacobian seen in active-set-sensitivity-differentiable-mpc.
Core Mechanism
General parametric PDE. Let u(t,x) be the latent solution and \mathcal N a (non)linear differential operator to the problem:
u_t + \mathcal{N}[u;\,\lambda] = 0, \quad x \in \Omega,\; t \in [0,T]
We replace u(t,x) with a neural network n_\theta(t,x) and define the residual network:
f_\theta(t,x) := \partial_t u_\theta + \mathcal{N}[u_\theta;\,\lambda]
In the forward problem, we fit the NN to minimize the residual, with fixed physical parameters \lambda. In the inverse problem, we will want to minimize the residual, optimising both the network weights and the physical quantities we want to estimate.
All derivatives in f_\theta come from autodiff of u_\theta w.r.t. its inputs. f_\theta shares weights with u_\theta but has a different computational graph (what does this mean?).
On a true PDE solution, f_\theta \equiv 0 (the residual is zero when the true solution is found).
Training loss (continuous time, forward).
\mathcal{L}(\theta) =
\underbrace{\frac{1}{N_u}\sum_i |u_\theta(t^i_u, x^i_u) - u^i|^2}_{\text{MSE}_u}
+ \underbrace{\frac{1}{N_f}\sum_j |f_\theta(t^j_f, x^j_f)|^2}_{\text{MSE}_f}
\text{MSE}_u fits initial/boundary data; \text{MSE}_f enforces the PDE at N_f collocation points.
To fit the NN parameters to data, optimise with L-BFGS for N_u \lesssim 10^3, or Adam for larger batches.
Discrete time variant. Apply an implicit Runge–Kutta scheme with q stages to
avoid needing collocation points globally:
u^{n+c_i} = u^n - \Delta t \sum_{j=1}^q a_{ij}\mathcal{N}[u^{n+c_j}],
\quad i = 1,\ldots,q
Place a multi-output NN prior on \{u^{n+c_1},\ldots,u^{n+c_q},u^{n+1}\}. The temporal truncation error is \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2q}); choosing q \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\log\epsilon / \log(\Delta t) drives it below machine precision, enabling very large time-steps (\Delta t = 0.8 on Allen–Cahn with relative L^2 error 6.99\times10^{-3}).
Inverse problem. Make \Theta_\text{phys} (Raissi calls it \lambda, but it clashes with the loss weight \lambda_\text{phys}) a learnable parameter:
\mathcal{L}(\theta,\Theta_\text{phys}) =
\frac{1}{N_u}\sum_i\|u_\theta(x^i,t^i) - u^\star_i\|^2
+ \lambda_\text{phys}\frac{1}{N_f}\sum_j\|\mathcal{N}[u_\theta;\Theta_\text{phys}](x^j,t^j)\|^2
+ R(\Theta_\text{phys})
R is a prior: \ell_2 around URDF nominal, or \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\|\Theta_\text{phys} - \Theta_\text{URDF}\|^2 (MAP estimator - bounds identification error in terms of the data Fisher information).
Identifiability via Fisher information.
\mathbf{F} = \sum_j \left(\partial_{\Theta}\mathcal{N}_j\right)
\left(\partial_{\Theta}\mathcal{N}_j\right)^\top
\Theta_\text{phys} is identifiable \Leftrightarrow \mathbf{F} has full rank near \Theta^\star.
Rank deficiency = the data does not excite enough modes to distinguish the parameters (persistence-of-excitation condition). The inverse problem becomes ill-posed not because the solver fails, but because the geometry of the data manifold collapses the relevant subspace.
SEA mapping. For a series-elastic actuator:
\tau_\text{real} = K_s(q_m - q) + D_s(\dot{q}_m - \dot{q}), \quad
J_m\ddot{q}_m = \tau_\text{cmd}(t-\delta) - \tau_\text{real}
\Theta_\text{phys} = (K_s, D_s, J_m, \delta); u_\theta absorbs the residual \Delta\tau on top of rigid-body inverse dynamics (friction nonlinearities). K_s vs D_s require distinct excitation: static hold (identifies K_s) and chirp (identifies D_s).
Results
Pending — challenge.py (Burgers’ viscosity recovery, \nu^\star = 0.01/\pi, N_u=100, N_f=10{,}000, 4-layer tanh MLP) is scaffolded but not yet trained.
Planned regimes:

(a) Baseline: \nu initialised at 0.1 (≈30× truth), clean data — expect
convergence to \nu^\star within \sim 5{,}000 Adam iterations + L-BFGS polish.
(b) Bad init: \nu_0 = 10 (≈3000× truth) — test for local minima.
(c) Data-starved: N_u = 20 — test Fisher-rank degradation.
(d) Noisy: 5% additive Gaussian noise on u — test MAP behaviour.

Will populate with \nu(\text{iter}) trajectories, final relative error, and a
short paragraph identifying which stress case breaks identifiability first.
Why It Matters
In humanoid system identification, the URDF values for SEA stiffness, damping,
and actuator delay are off by 10–30% from hardware. Standard RLS/UKF require a
fixed parametric model for the residual dynamics; the PINN NN absorbs whatever the
model misses while the small \Theta_\text{phys} vector carries the interpretable
physical values. Concretely: run a 10-minute chirp + push-recovery experiment on G1,
stack diverse trajectories to achieve PE across all entries of \mathbf{F}, and
recover (K_s, D_s, J_m, \delta) jointly without writing a closed-form residual model.
Compute is tractable at modest scale: Raissi reports Burgers’ forward solve
(N_u=100, N_f=10{,}000, 9-layer MLP, 20 neurons/layer) reaching relative
L^2 error 6.7\times 10^{-4} in 60s on a single Titan X — useful anchor
for sizing experiments.
Secondary use case: the discrete-time model lets you take \Delta t = 0.8\,\text{s}
time-steps for a stiff PDE (Allen–Cahn, Burgers shock) that would require millions of
explicit steps classically — effectively a high-order implicit integrator with free
differentiation.
Limitations

Collocation scaling. Continuous-time models need N_f to grow exponentially
in spatial dimension (curse of dimensionality). Sparse grid or QMC sampling only
partially mitigates this; the discrete-time variant sidesteps it entirely at the cost
of sequential stepping.
Loss conditioning. \nabla_\theta and \nabla_{\Theta_\text{phys}} scale
orders of magnitude differently. Fixed \lambda_\text{phys} leads to either
ignoring data (too large) or leaving \Theta_\text{phys} unidentified (too small).
Wang & Perdikaris 2021 adaptive gradient-norm weighting is necessary in practice.
No convergence guarantee. L-BFGS has no global convergence certificate; the
optimisation landscape is non-convex. Architectures that work for one PDE can fail
silently for another (Krishnapriyan et al., NeurIPS 2021).
Hidden state. If the true system has unobserved state (e.g. joint temperature
modulating friction), the inverse PINN converges to some average/envelope over that
state — not the true \Theta_\text{phys}.
Second-derivative autodiff cost. \mathcal{N} involving u_{xx} requires
differentiating through second derivatives of u_\theta; graph memory grows
with network depth. Separable PINNs / Hessian-free tricks required for deeper
architectures.

Counterweight — robustness. Raissi’s KdV inverse experiment recovers
\lambda_\text{true} = (1, 0.0025) as \lambda_\text{recovered} =
(0.999, 0.002\,4996) even at 1% noise on the data. The PDE residual term
acts as an infinite-dimensional regulariser, and noise on the data does not
propagate into \Theta_\text{phys} as long as identifiability holds. This is a
genuine advantage over RLS, which would absorb noise directly into the
parameter estimate.
Open Questions

For the G1 SEA problem: what is the minimal experiment duration and signal content
that makes \mathbf{F} full-rank above the IMU noise floor? Can this be designed
greedily (optimal experiment design)?
Is inverse PINN strictly better than UKF for SEA-ID, or is it UKF with a
non-parametric residual model? The key difference: UKF requires the residual to be
Gaussian; PINN’s NN absorbs arbitrary residuals — but at what sample cost?
Could a Hamiltonian/Lagrangian NN (Cranmer 2020) replace the URDF rigid-body model
entirely, and what do you lose in extrapolation compared to retaining the URDF
structure with PINN corrections?
Adaptive \lambda_\text{phys}(t) from gradient-norm balancing (Wang 2021): is
there a principled schedule rather than a heuristic, e.g. derived from the Fisher
information ratio between data and residual terms?
Diagnostic carry-over from deck 02: monitor \text{rank}(\mathbf{F}) during
training as a real-time identifiability alarm. When rank drops, halt and request
more excitation — instead of letting the loop converge to a manifold-projected
\Theta_\text{phys}. Is this standard in the SINDy/PINN-ID literature?

References

ADAM: optimization.cbe.cornell.edu/index.php
